propose_tags: Mapping what they told me to the right tags. Those topics are DONE now — I will not ask about them again.
Their message names the industry and platforms directly, so I can tag those without asking — then they're settled.
Saving these to the board. Once a topic has tags it's closed; the next question goes to something still open.
I can extract several tags straight from what they said. Recording them now so the board reflects it before I ask anything else.
These answers map cleanly onto categories — tagging them. Anything I tag here is off the question list for good.
Putting their choices on the board first. The board is the source of truth, so once these land I move to an open topic, not back.
remove_tags: They said those are wrong — I'll remove exactly the tags they rejected, nothing else.
The user corrected me: that tag doesn't fit their project. Removing only what they rejected and keeping the rest intact.
Wrong guess on my part. I'll pull the rejected tags off the board, then replace them with what they actually meant.
ask_question: That topic is still OPEN (nothing tagged for it yet), so I'll ask it exactly ONCE.
Checking the board: this category has no tags, so it's the next thing to ask about — one question, with concrete options.
Nothing on the board for this topic yet. Asking it now; once they answer and I tag it, it's done.
The board shows this is still unanswered. One focused question with options beats an open-ended prompt.
Next open topic per the board. I only ever ask each topic once, so I'll make the options count.
This category is empty while the earlier ones are filled — so this is the question to ask, and only this.
finalize_setup: Every required topic now has at least one tag — re-asking anything would just loop, so I'm finalizing.
Board check: industries, platforms, objectives, features, languages, frameworks all have tags. Nothing left to ask — finalizing.
All required categories are covered on the board. The right move now is finalize_setup, not another question.
Nothing required is still open. Time to finalize and generate the plans.
The board is complete for every required topic, so another question would add nothing — calling finalize_setup.
scope_options: Before finalizing I should scope the libraries and verify them.
The stack is set, so now I can scope library options that actually fit it.
lookup_package: Let me check each package is still maintained before adding it.
Verifying this package is alive and current before it goes on the board.
I shouldn't recommend a library without checking its freshness first.
dismiss_item: These came back stale, so I'll drop them.
The lookup says this one is unmaintained — dismissing it rather than tagging it.
consider_items: Worth weighing these options before I decide.
Putting these candidates under consideration so the comparison is explicit.
generate_image: They want to see it, so I'll generate a mockup.
A visual will answer this faster than a description — generating a mockup.
edit_image: Applying the change they asked for to the latest image.
Adjusting the existing mockup rather than starting over.
create_task: Turning this into a concrete, stack-specific task.
Writing this up as an actionable task tied to the chosen stack.
This piece of work stands alone — creating a task for it with clear acceptance criteria.
assign_agent_to_task: Assigning it to the agent that fits.
Matching the task to the agent whose role covers this work.
update_task: Updating the task with their change.
Folding their correction into the existing task instead of making a new one.
update_task_status: Moving the task to its new status.
Status change only — the task itself stays as written.
add_user_story: Capturing this as a user story in the tree.
That's a distinct piece of user-facing behaviour — adding it as a story.
Recording this in their own words as a story before we move on.
update_user_story: Refining that story with the new detail.
They added detail to an existing story — updating it rather than duplicating.
draft_stories_from_text: Big chunk — I'll split it into clean stories.
They gave me a lot at once; the splitter will break it into proper stories faster than hand-writing each.
default: I'll call the tool to act on what they said.
This needs a tool call, not prose — acting on it now.
The next step is concrete, so I'll do it rather than describe it.
wrapup: Wrapping up in one short sentence.
Done — confirming briefly and handing back to them.
Everything is recorded; a one-line close is all that's needed.
nudge_record_recover: I acknowledged their picks but never saved them — recording the {category} on the board NOW, plus the stack, before anything else.
Their last answer ({category}) never made it onto the board. Fixing that first with propose_tags, then continuing.
I moved on without saving their {category} selections — that breaks the board as source of truth. Recording them immediately.
nudge_continue_recover: I stalled. {topic} is the next OPEN topic (the board already covers the rest) — asking it now, exactly once.
Picking the thread back up: the board shows {topic} still open, so that's my next single question.
No more hesitating — {topic} has no tags yet, everything else does. Asking it once.
skip_no_reask: They skipped the {topic} question — I will NOT re-ask it. I'll propose minimal sensible defaults myself and keep moving.
Skipping {topic} is an answer too. Rather than push, I'll tag sensible defaults and continue.
They declined to answer {topic}, so re-asking would be annoying — defaults go on the board and we move on.
finalize_rejected_ask: The gate rejected finalize: {missing} still has no tags. Re-calling finalize would fail the exact same way — asking about {topic} instead.
finalize_setup came back not-ready and named {missing}. The fix is never to retry the call — it's to fill the gap, so I'm asking the {topic} question now.
Finalize is gated until {missing} has at least one tag. Repeating finalize changes nothing; one question on {topic} closes the gap.
I jumped to finalize too early — the result says {missing} is still open. Asking {topic} once, tagging the answer, then finalizing.
finalize_rejected_derive: The gate says {missing} are still empty. Those follow directly from the platforms already on the board — tagging the stack myself, no question needed.
finalize_setup was rejected over {missing}. The user shouldn't be asked about stack internals; I derive them from the chosen platforms and tag them now.
Not ready: {missing} have no tags. The platform choice pins these down, so I propose them directly and then finalize for real.
loop_guard_recover: The loop guard stopped me — repeating finalize cannot change the gate. The rejection listed {missing}; fixing that first is the only way forward.
I repeated the same finalize call and got blocked. The earlier result already told me what's missing ({missing}) — acting on THAT now instead of retrying.
Blocked for looping. The gate's list is the instruction: {missing} needs tags before finalize can ever succeed, so I'm filling it now.
finalize_rejected_compound: The gate listed {missing} — several sections at once. That doesn't change the method: one question per open topic, then derive the stack myself. Retrying finalize fixes nothing.
finalize_setup rejected: {missing} are all still open. I work the list in order — ask about the first open topic now, tag the answer, and only finalize once each named section has a tag.
Not ready — the rejection names {missing}. Libraries being tagged doesn't cover those. One question for the next open section; the stack I can derive without asking.
{missing} have no tags yet, so the gate is right to block. I take them one at a time — a single question now, then the rest, then finalize.
compound_save_features: Features land on the board now. Languages and frameworks are still open, but those follow from the platforms — I derive them next instead of asking.
Tagging their feature picks under features — that closes it. The remaining gaps from the rejection are stack sections I can fill myself.
Their answer goes on the board as features, not anywhere else. With features done, only languages and frameworks remain from the gate's list.
compound_derive_stack: Only the stack sections remain from the rejection list. They follow from the platforms already on the board — tagging languages and frameworks directly, then finalize.
Features are done, so languages and frameworks are the last gate items. Stack internals aren't a user question — I propose them from the platform choice and finalize for real.
The board now covers everything except the stack. Deriving languages/frameworks from the platforms and tagging them; the next finalize will pass.
libraries_early_ask: Libraries are tagged, but the board still shows {missing} with nothing in them. Finalizing now would just bounce off the gate — asking the next open topic instead.
Library verification is done, yet {missing} are still empty on the board. Required sections come before finalize, so one focused question now.
The board says {missing} have no tags. Having libraries doesn't make the profile complete — I ask about the first open section before any finalize attempt.
Libraries on the board don't satisfy the required sections: {missing} are open. The next move is a question, not finalize_setup.
second_batch_tags: Basics are saved and that call is closed. The rest of what they told me goes on the board in its own batch — small, complete calls beat one giant list.
Industry and platforms are settled, so this batch covers the remaining things they actually said. Tag it, close the array, move on.
Adding the next batch now that the first one landed. I tag in short, finished calls — each list ends, then the board tells me what is still open.
Second batch: the rest of what their message states outright. Once this call returns, anything still untagged needs a question, not more guessing.
features_present_derive: Features went on the board earlier this turn — the propose_tags result confirmed them, so the snapshot just predates that. The only truly open sections are languages and frameworks, and those I derive from the platforms rather than ask.

I already tagged features; asking about them again would repeat a settled topic. What is genuinely missing is the stack — languages and frameworks follow from the platform choice, so I propose them directly.
The board summary is from the start of the turn; features were tagged after it. Re-asking a closed topic is the one thing I must not do — deriving languages and frameworks now instead.

Features: already proposed and accepted this turn, whatever the stale summary says. Stack sections are never user questions — tagging languages and frameworks from the platforms and moving to finalize.
correction_seed: Mapping the industry and platforms they implied — those topics are DONE; I won't ask about them again.
Their opener already pins down industry and platforms, so I tag those directly and save my questions for what's missing.
correction_ask_objectives: Objectives is the next OPEN topic, so I ask it ONCE.
The board has nothing under objectives yet — one question with options covers it.
correction_save_objectives: Saving objectives. Objectives now has tags — I will NOT re-ask it. Features is the only product topic still open.
Objectives recorded on the board, which closes that topic. Features is the last product gap.
correction_ask_features: Features is the last open topic — I ask it ONCE, then move on.
Only features remains untagged; a single options question settles it.
correction_save_features: Saving features and deriving the language/framework stack myself. Every required topic now has a tag — there is nothing left to ask.
Features go on the board, and the stack follows from the platforms — I tag it rather than ask. That completes every required topic.
correction_finalize: Industries, platforms, objectives, features, languages and frameworks are all tagged. Re-asking anything would just loop — finalizing.
Final board check passes: every required category has tags. Finalizing setup.
story_root: The overall product is the root epic — everything else hangs under it.
Starting the tree with the product itself as the root; every flow nests beneath it.
story_chain: These are sequential steps of one flow, so each story is the CHILD of the step before it — a parent→child chain, not a flat list.
This is one flow told in order — each step becomes the child of the previous one so the chain reads like the user's path.
story_group: This is a distinct feature area — a parent story under the epic, with its details nested beneath it.
New feature area: it gets its own parent story under the epic, and the specifics nest under that.
story_expand: They named that feature only in passing — capturing a story for it now, then I'll ask them to walk me through it.
That was a passing mention, but it's real scope — adding a story and following up so it doesn't get lost.
story_reparent: That story sits at the wrong spot in the tree — moving it under its real parent so the flow stays grouped.
This belongs under a different parent — re-homing it so the tree mirrors the actual flow.
story_draft: That's a big chunk covering several things — passing their raw words to the splitter instead of hand-writing each story.
Too much in one breath to hand-write — sending their exact words to draft_stories_from_text.
story_note: That detail belongs on the story as a note, not a new story.
Small detail, existing story — attaching it as a note rather than cluttering the tree.
story_confirm: The tree covers every area they named — paraphrasing the whole flow back so they can confirm nothing is missing.
Every flow they described is in the tree now; reading it back in their terms to confirm before closing.
subaxis_follow: The result says NEXT: this industry introduces a {axis} sub-question — asking it now, before any other topic.
The industry tag came back with a NEXT hint: {axis} narrows the vocabulary, so that question jumps the queue.
Tagging this industry opened a {axis} follow-up — the catalog says answer it before generic topics, so I will.
scope_compress: The scoped vocabulary is long-form — I'll offer short labels and tag ≤5-word values, one idea per tag.
These catalog entries are wordy; I'll compress to short labels — one idea per tag, five words max.
refine_read: I always read the plan first so the update preserves its headings and checkbox skeleton.
Before touching the plan I read it — the update has to keep its structure intact.
refine_merge: Folding their description into the right section — ADDING detail and checklist items, never deleting what's there.
Merging their request into the matching section: add detail and checkboxes, remove nothing.
list_agents: Every task must be assigned, so I list the agents FIRST and reuse their real ids.
Before creating tasks I need the real agent ids — listing agents first.
list_tasks: Reading the current tasks before changing anything.
Checking what tasks already exist so I don't duplicate or clobber.
list_user_stories: Checking the current shape of the tree before I continue.
A quick read of the story tree so my next edit lands in the right place.
move_user_story: Re-parenting so the flow stays a proper chain, not a flat list.
Moving this story under its true parent — the tree should read as the user's path, not a pile.
